front: What is an OS?
back: A program that acts as an intermediary between user and hardware

front: What is virtualization?
back: A method that allows operating systems to run applications within other OS'es

Traps (or exceptions) are another form of interrupts, and is software generated. Why are they caused?
back: Because of errors or by a specific request from a user program that an operating-system service be performed by executing a special operation called a system call.

front: What does multiprogramming do?
back: Increases CPU utilization, as well as keeping users satisfied by organizing programs so that the CPU always has one to execute.

front: What are programs in execution called in a multiprogrammed system?
What is the main difficulty that a programmer must overcome in writing an operating system for a real-time environment?
back: Keeping the operating system within the fixed time constraints of a real-time system.

front: How does the distinction between kernel mode and user mode function as a rudimentary form of protection (security)?
back: It provides a form of security, in that certain instructions can not be executed unless in kernel mode. Similarly, hardware devices can only be accessed when the program is in kernel mode.

front: Some CPUs provide for more than two modes of operation. What are two possible uses of these multiple modes?
back: Different kinds of user modes. Specific kernel modes, for example if you want a mode to only be able to use USB

front: Give two reasons why caches are useful. What problems do they solve? What problems do they cause? If a cache can be made as large as the device for which it is caching (for instance, a cache as large as a disk), why not make it that large and eliminate the device?
back: Caches are useful when two or more components need to exchange data, and the components perform transfers at differing speeds.

front: How do clustered systems differ from multiprocessor systems? What is required for two machines belonging to a cluster to cooperate to provide a highly available service?
back: Clustered systems use messages to communicate, while processors in a multiprocessor system could communicate using shared memory

What is the difference between kernel modules and applications?
back: Two main differences: Kernel Modules are exclusively event-driven and a kernel modules exit function always has to clean up from the init function.

front: What is the consequence of a fault in a kernel module, and how is it different from a fault in a normal application?
back: In a normal program a debugger can catch faults, while with kernel modules the kernel, or maybe even the entire system fails completely.

front: Do kernel modules run in user space?
back: No, they run in kernel space. Normal applications run in user space.

front: What is the difference between insmod and modprobe?
back: Modprobe loads any other modules required by the module you want to load, but only look sin the standard installed modules directory.

front: Do usb mice and sound cards use character devices or block devices?
What does the major number in /dev signify?
back: A unique way oif identifying a driver which can be associated with a particular device device.

front: What does the minor number in /dev signify?
back: The minor number corresponds to the number of instances of the device. A minor number starts at 0 and goes to the number of instaces minus one. Each instance will have its own minor number.

front: What is a race condition?
back: Race conditions are a result of uncontrolled access to shared data.

front: What is locking or mutual exclusion?
back: A technique for access management that makes sure only one thread of execution can manipulate a shared resource at any time.

front: What is a spinlock?
back: A lock that forces a process that is trying to access the lock to wait in a loop while repeatedly checking if the lock is available, until the lock is available.
